mnemonics: CPU Time = IC * CPI * CT (Think: Instruction Count, Clocks Per Instruction, Clock Cycle Time. A simple product of three factors.)
keyPoints: CPU execution time is the product of Instruction Count (IC), Cycles per Instruction (CPI), and Clock Cycle Time (CT).
Improving performance means decreasing the value of any one or more of the three factors in the equation.

The equation highlights the fundamental trade-offs in computer architecture: reducing instruction count (better ISA/compiler) vs. reducing CPI (better microarchitecture) vs. reducing clock cycle time (faster hardware).
commonMistakes: Confusing CPI (Cycles per Instruction) with its reciprocal, IPC (Instructions per Cycle). A lower CPI is better, while a higher IPC is better.
Forgetting that Clock Cycle Time (CT) is the reciprocal of Clock Rate (Frequency). Performance is improved by decreasing CT or increasing clock rate.
Applying the equation to a program with multiple instruction types (e.g., loads, adds, branches) without using the weighted average CPI: CPU Time = (Σ (IC_i * CPI_i)) * CT
